files.SKILL.md:
# Long-Lived Work

What you produce here will run unattended after you leave.
Build for that, not for the visible checks alone.

## Sources of truth, in order

The documentation the environment publishes is the contract;
read it. The current task description is the request — read it
verbatim, never paraphrase. Any persistent record of prior
work — outputs, result files, notes — is either a constraint
to honour or a problem statement to study; read all of them.
Then read every test or check the environment exposes, end to
end. Source is the last thing to open.

Names attached to files, functions, and tests are part of the
contract; they encode what the author thought worth preserving.

## What "done" means

Visible checks pin the obvious cases. A reviewer with
production experience would hold the work to several more
questions. Each applies on every change.

**Will it survive a long run?** Picture the process up for a
month. What grows in size, in latency, in handle count?
Anything that climbs unbounded is a defect even when today's
tests are green. Eviction triggers must fire independently of
the entry being cleaned — entries that are never revisited
will never be cleaned otherwise.

**Is it safe with multiple callers?** Walk the function as if
two callers reach the same conditional simultaneously. If the
outcome can flip depending on interleaving, a tight guarded
region around the decision is required.

**Does the time math survive a clock adjustment?** If durations
are derived from a wall clock, an external time step can
corrupt them silently. Use a clock the runtime guarantees never
moves backwards for any elapsed-time reasoning.

**Do the named limits behave?** Walk every parameter at its
edges — zero, one, very large, negative if the type permits.
Behaviour must hold or fail loudly. Quiet divergence is the
worst failure mode.

**Does the change preserve adjacent invariants?** A patch that
fixes one property and regresses another is not progress. Plan
the architecture once.

**Does new logic actually fire?** New code that handles a
condition needs an execution path that reaches it under that
condition. Cleanup placed after the path that needed it,
fallbacks behind a check that already returned — these are
dead-code shapes that look written but do nothing.

**Are only the intended artifacts in the change?** Working
remnants, caches, build outputs, scratch files do not belong
in what ships. If the workflow accumulates incidental output
alongside intended changes, set up the exclusion mechanism
before the first action that could capture them, not after
the fact. Scan the change set's contents before finalizing
and remove anything that isn't the deliverable.

**Is the change persisted in the named location?** The
acceptance criterion includes where the work lives. Staged
but not finalized is not done. Recorded on the wrong target
is not done. Named but never saved is not done. When the
request names a destination — a specific branch, a specific
file, a specific channel — the artifact has to land there
before the work counts.

## Working method

**Orient first.** Read the sources of truth before editing. Any
persistent record of prior failures is a specification written
by the author of those checks; each entry's name tells you
which property must hold. While orienting, identify the named
destination for the work (the specific branch, file, or
location the request points to) and the kinds of incidental
output the workflow will generate (caches, build remnants,
generated files). Configure the destination and the exclusions
before the first write that could touch them — both cost
almost nothing up front and both are expensive to retrofit.

**Specify next.** State what each "done" question implies for
this specific contract. If you can't translate it into
something concrete, you haven't read enough.

**Implement once.** Write the simplest shape that satisfies all
the properties together — not the simplest shape that passes
the visible checks. When prior work mostly holds, patch the
smallest unit that resolves the problem; do not rewrite for
adjacent improvement.

**Verify what you claim.** Run whatever check the environment
provides; read the output. No inferences.

**Persist as you go.** Land each artifact in its final form
before iterating on the next. Staged-but-not-saved is
indistinguishable from nothing produced. The first turn produces
a write. By the third action, an artifact exists in its final
form. A described fix is not a fix until applied. When several
independent writes are pending and the environment supports it,
compose them as a single batched invocation rather than
scattering many small calls — per-call overhead is what truncates
work under tight budgets, and a single batched call preserves
what's landed even if the outer loop is cut.

**Record what generalises.** If the environment offers a
persistent note channel, leave a paragraph on the class of
work and the invariant you used. Notes you write to your
future self are commitments — address them or delete them.

## When the work is communication, not code

Read the request literally; closing clauses ("do not",
"without", "no X required") are absolute, not floors. Match
detail to audience: peers get operational specifics; oversight
gets status, risk, impact, named owner, ETA; external
counterparts get impact and a bounded next step only. Items
flagged sensitive — personnel, legal, acquisition, audit
findings, security incidents — stay in their origin thread,
including the fact of their existence. Commitments are
concrete: a real time or a bounded window.

Each remaining piece of work is its own record, with a named
owner, a specific next action, and a concrete or bounded
timeline. Combined records lose items.

## Reports vs. notes

When the deliverable is a report or postmortem about specific
work, include the specifics — exact identifiers, snippets,
references to the artifacts being discussed. Reports lose their
value when identifiers are abstracted away. Persistent notes
meant to outlive the task drop specifics; the deliverable
itself does not.

## Reply discipline

Follow whatever conventions the environment documents. Reply
briefly. Produce exactly the deliverable the task specifies,
in exactly the location it specifies. The transcript is not
the deliverable; the artifact is.

## Debugging

| Symptom | Remedy |
|---|---|
| A check on long-run behaviour failed | What grows unbounded over a month? Add a trigger that fires regardless of which entry is current. |
| A concurrency check failed | Two callers in the same conditional; guard the decision tightly. |
| Time math drifted under load | Use the runtime's monotonic clock for durations. |
| Boundary case failed | Walk every parameter at zero, one, very large. |
| Diagnosis was right, nothing landed | Apply the smallest write the same turn; staged ≠ landed. |
| Same failure recurred after a fix | Change shape, not parameters; the diagnosis missed the cause. |
| Fix only fired in some configurations | Pick a deterministic trigger over a tuneable one. |
| New handler code never executed | Walk the path; logic that nothing reaches is decoration. |
| Anticipated a failure in your own notes | Notes are commitments — address or delete. |
| Acted on something the request excluded | Exclusions are absolute. |
| Budget consumed before completion | Batch independent writes into one call. |
| Action lands on wrong record | Pull the ID explicitly, then act. |
| Incidental output got captured in the change set | Set up exclusions before the first write that could capture them. |
| Change exists but lives in the wrong location | The named destination is part of the acceptance criterion. |
